quality.rationale: 需利用光子方向洛伦兹变换求立体角雅可比，多步推导结合相对论聚束效应物理背景

origin.question:
[文件 1: 1_s1_1.9.md]

Problem 1.9. Suppose that an observer at rest with respect to the fixed distant stars sees an isotropic distribution of stars. That is, in any solid angle $\mathrm{d}\Omega$ he sees $\mathrm{dN} = \mathrm{N}(\mathrm{d}\Omega /4\pi)$ stars, where $\mathbf{N}$ is the total number of stars he can see.
Suppose now that another observer (whose rest frame is $S'$ ) is moving at a relativistic velocity $ eta$ in the $e_x$ direction. What is the distribution of stars seen by this observer? Specifically, what is the distribution function $P(	heta', \phi')$ such that the number of stars seen by this observer in his solid angle $d\Omega'$ is $P(	heta', \phi') d\Omega'$ ? Check to see that $\int_{	ext{sphere}} P(	heta', \phi') d\Omega' = N$ , and check that $P(	heta', \phi') 	o  rac{N}{4\pi}$ as $ eta 	o 0$ . Where will the observer see the stars "bunch up"?
origin.answer: $P(\\theta', \\phi') = \\frac{N}{4\\pi} \\frac{1 - \\beta^2}{(1 - \\beta \\cos \\theta')^2}$；恒星向运动前方聚集（前向半锥角为 $\\cos^{-1}\\beta$）
origin.solution:
If $\overline{	heta}$ is the direction of the motion of a photon from star to observer then the star is observed at angle $	heta = \pi - \overline{	heta}$ . The transformation of photon directions was derived in the previous problem. From that result we have here
$$
\cos \theta = \frac {\cos \theta^{\prime} - \beta}{1 - \beta \cos \theta^{\prime}}.
$$
The number of stars observed in solid angle $\mathrm{d}\Omega$ is then
$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d} \mathbf {N} = \mathrm {N d} \Omega / 4 \pi = (\mathrm {N} / 4 \pi) 2 \pi \mathrm {d} (\cos \theta) \\ = \frac {1}{2} N \frac {d (\cos \theta)}{d (\cos \theta^{\prime})} d (\cos \theta^{\prime}) \\ = \frac {1}{2} N \frac {\left(1 - \beta^{2}\right)}{\left(1 - \beta \cos \theta^{\prime}\right)^{2}} d (\cos \theta^{\prime}). \\ \end{array}
$$
Since $\mathrm{d}\mathbf{N} = 2\pi \mathbf{P}(	heta', \phi') \mathrm{d}(\cos 	heta')$ the distribution in $S'$ is
$$
\mathrm {P} (\theta^{\prime}, \phi^{\prime}) = \frac {\mathrm {N}}{4 \pi} \frac {(1 - \beta^{2})}{(1 - \beta \cos \theta^{\prime})^{2}} .
$$
Checks: $ eta ightarrow 0\mathrm{P}(\theta^{\prime},\phi^{\prime})ightarrow \mathbf {N} / 4\pi = \mathrm {P}(\theta ,\phi)$
$$
\int_{\text {s p h e r e}} \mathrm {P} \left(\theta^{\prime}, \phi^{\prime}\right) \mathrm {d} \Omega = \frac {\mathrm {N} \left(1 - \beta^{2}\right)}{4 \pi} \int_{- 1}^{+ 1} \frac {2 \pi \mathrm {d} \cos \theta^{\prime}}{\left(1 - \beta \cos \theta^{\prime}\right)^{2}} = \mathrm {N}.
$$
You can easily verify that half the stars are contained between $	heta' = 0$ and $	heta_{\frac{1}{2}}' = \cos^{-1}\beta < \pi/2$ . Thus the stars "bunch up" in the forward direction. For $ eta \approx 1$ , $	heta_{\frac{1}{2}}' \approx [2(1 - \beta)]^{\frac{1}{2}}$ and the bunching is pronounced.
